perturbation.original_text: 1.9 Business Day - Monday through Friday, excluding days on which Bank of America is not open for business in the United States of America.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.9 Business Day - Any day of the week is considered a business day.
perturbation.explanation: This modification broadens the definition of 'Business Day' to include weekends and holidays, conflicting with the standard legal definition.
perturbation.law_explanation: By redefining 'Business Day', the contract potentially impacts overtime calculations and other time-sensitive obligations under the FLSA, creating uncertainty and contradicting the law's intent regarding standard work weeks.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 541 overtime regulations. The Overtime Calculator Advisor computes the amount of overtime pay due in a sample pay period based on information from the user. The Child Labor Rules Advisor answers questions about the FLSA’s youth employment provisions, including at what age young people can work and the jobs they can perform. The Section 14(c) Advisor helps users understand the special minimum wage requirements for workers with disabilities. Presentations Comprehensive FLSA Presentation (Microsoft PowerPoint) Executive, Administrative, and Professional Exemption Presentation (Microsoft PowerPoint) Applicable Laws and Regulations Law Fair Labor Standards Act Regulations 29 CFR Chapter V Civil Money Penalty Inflation Adjustments Starting in 2016, agencies across the federal government must adjust their penalties for inflation each year. Below is a table that reflects the adjustments that have occurred for penalties under this statute. For more information on the penalty adjustments, go here . perturbation.justification: The modification redefines 'Business Day' to include weekends and holidays, conflicting with the standard legal definition, potentially impacting overtime calculations. The Law Explanation generated by the LLM asserts this could contradict the FLSA's intent regarding standard work weeks. The scraped snippet references the Fair Labor Standards Act and overtime regulations which supports that the definition of business day can impact overtime calculations. The altered definition directly opposes the typical understanding of a business day in a legal context, thus creating a legal contradiction according to the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.10 Consumer Information - any record about an individual, whether in paper. electronic. or other form, that is a consumer report as such term is defined in the Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 USC 1681 et seq.) or is derived from a consumer report and that is maintained or otherwise possessed by or on behalf of Bank of America for a business purpose. Consumer Information also means a compilation of such records. The term does not include any record that does not identify an individual.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.10 Consumer Information - any information about a consumer, regardless of its source or identifiability. This definition supersedes any conflicting definitions in the Fair Credit Reporting Act.
perturbation.explanation: This redefinition broadens 'Consumer Information' beyond the scope defined in the FCRA, potentially subjecting non-report data to stricter privacy obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCRA explicitly defines what constitutes a 'consumer report.' By expanding the definition of 'Consumer Information' beyond this, the contract creates ambiguity about which data is subject to FCRA regulations, contradicting the statute.

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Consumer Information' to include 'any information about a consumer, regardless of its source or identifiability,' and explicitly states this supersedes conflicting definitions in the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA). This directly contradicts the FCRA, which has specific definitions for consumer reports as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which is under SUBCHAPTER III—CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES and includes sections such as '1681a. Definitions; rules of construction.' By attempting to broaden the definition beyond what the FCRA allows and then stating it supersedes the act, the contract creates a clear contradiction and attempts to circumvent the legal framework of the FCRA. The changed text removes the limitation that consumer information must be a consumer report as defined by the FCRA, leading to potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 27.1 The term "Confidential Information" shall mean this Agreement and all data, trade secrets, business information and other information of any kind whatsoever that a Party ("Discloser'') discloses, in writing, orally, visually or in any other medium, to the other Party ("Recipient") or to which Recipient obtains access and that relates to Discloser or, in the case of Supplier, to Bank of America or its Representatives, customers, third-party vendors or licensors. Confidential Information includes Associate Information, Customer information and Consumer information, as defined in the Section entitled ''Definitions." A "writing" shall include an electronic transfer of information by e-mail, over the internet or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 27.1 The term "Confidential Information" shall mean any information, regardless of its nature, source, or prior disclosure, exchanged between the parties. This definition includes publicly available information and information already known to the recipient. No information shall be excluded from this definition.
perturbation.explanation: This modification redefines 'Confidential Information' to include publicly available information, which contradicts established legal principles regarding trade secrets and confidential data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines a 'trade secret' as information that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep secret. By including publicly available information in the definition of Confidential Information, this clause contradicts the core requirement of secrecy under the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract redefines 'Confidential Information' to include publicly available information, directly contradicting the definition of a 'trade secret' under 18 U.S. Code § 1839. The law snippet specifies that a trade secret derives its value from 'not being generally known' and requires the owner to take 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret.' By including publicly available information, the changed text negates the secrecy requirement, creating a legal contradiction because information generally available cannot be a trade secret, making the changed text unenforceable.
